blocks.x: A factor of a number is a side of a rectangle with that area, every row the same length, nothing left over. Factors travel in pairs, since each side has a matching partner on the opposite edge whose product is the original number.
A class arranges 24 floor cushions into one full rectangular block, every row the same length, no cushion left over. How many different row counts work?
hints: A number of rows works only if you can split 24 into that many equal rows with nothing left over. Try 1 row, then 2, then 3, and keep going, checking each time whether 24 divides up cleanly.
blocks.sol: A number of rows works exactly when it splits 24 into equal rows with nothing left over, which is the same as saying that number divides 24 evenly. Walk upward and write each working count beside the row length it forces. $$1\times24,\quad 2\times12,\quad 3\times8,\quad 4\times6$$ Each arrangement names two numbers at once, the rows and the row length, and their product is always 24. Reading both numbers out of every rectangle gives the full list of row counts that work. $$1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 6,\ 8,\ 12,\ 24$$ These are precisely the numbers that divide 24 evenly. We call them the factors, or divisors, of 24, and there are $$\boxed{8}$$ of them. Quick check, the 4-by-6 rectangle holds \(4\times6=24\) cushions, exactly the pile we started with.

blocks.x: Find all the factors of 48 by writing factor pairs \(1\times48, 2\times24, \ldots\) and stopping once the two sides of a pair would cross. How many factors does 48 have?
hints: The moment you find a small side like 3, the matching side is forced, since 3 rows must each be \(48\div3\) tiles long. So every small factor you find hands you a large one for free.

Watch the two sides of each pair close in on each other. Once the next side you would test is bigger than the partner it would pair with, every grid past that point is just one you have already seen turned on its side.
blocks.sol: Start at 1 and climb, writing each factor with the partner it forces, since \(d\) rows of length \(48\div d\) is the same wall. $$1\times48,\quad 2\times24,\quad 3\times16,\quad 4\times12,\quad 6\times8$$ Each small side fixes its larger partner, so the factors come out already sorted into matched pairs whose product is 48. Now watch where to stop. After 6 we try 7, but 7 does not divide 48. The next number that divides, 8, is already standing on the right of the pair \(6\times8\). Once the side you are about to test would be larger than the partner it would pair with, you have already met every rectangle, just mirrored, so the search is finished. Reading both numbers out of every pair gives all the factors. $$1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 6,\ 8,\ 12,\ 16,\ 24,\ 48$$ There are $$\boxed{10}$$ of them. Quick check, the middle pair \(6\times8=48\), and we stopped right after 6 because 8 was already accounted for.

blocks.x: If \(6\) is a factor of \(36\), then \(36\) is a multiple of \(6\), it sits exactly \(6, 12, 18, 24, 30, 36\) steps out from \(0\). Factor and multiple are one relationship read from its two ends.
A train starts at stone 0 and rolls forward exactly 7 stones at a time. How many of the four stones 28, 35, 50, 63 does it land on?
hints: Keep adding \(7\). \(7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42, 49, 56, 63\). Which of the four stones appear on that list?
blocks.sol: Skip forward by sevens and watch where the markers fall. $$7,\ 14,\ 21,\ 28,\ 35,\ 42,\ 49,\ 56,\ 63,\ \ldots$$ Stones \(28, 35,\) and \(63\) all appear, but \(50\) does not, since the train steps from \(49\) straight to \(56\) and skips right over it. The landings are exactly the stones reached by a whole number of \(7\)-steps. $$28=4\times7,\qquad 35=5\times7,\qquad 63=9\times7$$ Each landing reads two ways. Take \(28\). Read one direction, \(7\) is a factor of \(28\), since \(28=4\times7\). Read the other direction, \(28\) is a multiple of \(7\), since it is four \(7\)-steps out from \(0\). The same double reading holds for \(35\) and \(63\). Stone \(50\) gets neither, because \(7\) is not a factor of it and it is not a multiple of \(7\). The train lands on $$\boxed{28,\ 35,\ 63}.$$

blocks.x: For each pair, decide whether the first is a factor of the second, a multiple, both, or neither. (a) 4 and 12 (b) 15 and 5 (c) 8 and 8 (d) 6 and 9
hints: For each pair, ask two separate questions. Does the first number divide the second evenly, and is the first number itself a whole-number times the second?
\(a\) is a factor of \(b\) when \(b = a \times (\text{whole number})\). \(a\) is a multiple of \(b\) when \(a = b \times (\text{whole number})\). A pair can satisfy both, one, or neither.
Try the pair where both numbers are equal. Does \(8\) divide \(8\), and is \(8\) a whole-number times \(8\)?
accept: a factor b multiple c both d neither
blocks.sol: Each pair asks one question twice, once in each direction. The first number is a factor of the second when the second is a whole-number times the first, and the first is a multiple of the second when the first is a whole-number times the second. (a) \(4\) and \(12\). Is \(12\) a whole-number times \(4\)? Yes, \(12 = 4 \times 3\), so \(4\) is a factor of \(12\). Going the other way, \(4\) is not \(12\) times any whole number, so it is not a multiple of \(12\). (b) \(15\) and \(5\). Is \(15\) a whole-number times \(5\)? Yes, \(15 = 5 \times 3\), so \(15\) is a multiple of \(5\). It cannot be a factor of \(5\), since a factor never exceeds the number it divides. (c) \(8\) and \(8\). Here \(8 = 8 \times 1\) read both ways at once. \(8\) divides \(8\) evenly, so it is a factor, and \(8\) is one copy of \(8\), so it is a multiple. A number is always both a factor and a multiple of itself. (d) \(6\) and \(9\). Does \(6\) divide \(9\) evenly? No, \(9 \div 6\) leaves a remainder. Is \(6\) a whole-number times \(9\)? No, \(6\) is smaller than \(9\). So the answer is neither. Notice that \(4\) and \(12\) tell the same story as \(15\) and \(5\), just told from opposite ends. Whenever one number is a factor of another, that other number is automatically a multiple of the first. Factor and multiple are not two relationships, they are one relationship read in two directions.

blocks.x: Factor and multiple are one relationship, read from two ends. A whole number $f$ is a factor (or divisor) of $n$ when $n = f \times k$ for some whole number $k$, equivalently when $n \div f$ leaves no remainder, equivalently when $n$ objects fill a rectangle with $f$ along one side. Turn the same arrow around and $n$ is a multiple of $f$, sitting $k$ whole steps of size $f$ out from $0$. So $f$ is a factor of $n$ exactly when $n$ is a multiple of $f$. Read it backward you say factor, read it forward you say multiple. Factors also travel in pairs, since each side $f$ is matched by its partner $n \div f$, and the two multiply to $n$.
The factors of 36 pair as \(1\times36, 2\times18, 3\times12, 4\times9, 6\times6\). Does 49 have an odd or even number of factors? What about 35?
hints: Every pair so far has used two different side lengths. Look hard at \(6\times6\). How many new factors does that arrangement actually contribute?
\(49=7\times7\) and \(35=5\times7\). In which one does a side pair with itself?
blocks.ans: 36 has nine factors, an odd count, because the 6-by-6 square self-pairs; 49 has an odd count, 35 has an even count
blocks.sol: Walk the pairs that fill a \(36\)-tile rectangle, each side handing you its partner. $$1\times36,\quad 2\times18,\quad 3\times12,\quad 4\times9,\quad 6\times6$$ The first four pairs each name two different factors, eight factors so far. The last one is different. The square \(6\times6\) uses the same number on both sides, so \(6\) is its own partner. It adds a single new factor, not a fresh pair. Counting them all, $$1,2,3,4,6,9,12,18,36,$$ gives \(\boxed{9}\) factors, an odd total. Here is the reason. Factors come two at a time, a small side and its larger partner, so they would always count up to an even number. The only way to break that is for one factor to pair with itself, \(d\times d=n\), which happens exactly when \(n\) is a perfect square. That lone self-paired factor is counted once, tipping the total to odd. So \(49=7\times7\) self-pairs at \(7\) and has an odd number of factors, while \(35\) splits only into the distinct pairs \(1\times35\) and \(5\times7\), four factors, an even count. Quick check on \(35\). Its factors are \(1,5,7,35\), and \(4\) is indeed even.

blocks.cap: A factor of a number is a way to fill a full rectangle, and the two side lengths form a factor pair. On the left, \(3\times4=12\) uses two different partners. On the right, the square \(6\times6=36\) uses one number on both sides, so the two partners have become the same factor. That single self-paired side, the gold corner on the diagonal, is the lone unpaired factor that gives a perfect square its odd total.

An odd factor count is the fingerprint of a perfect square. Almost every factor pairs with a different partner, so factors come two at a time and the count is even. The one way to break that is for a factor to pair with itself, $f \times f = n$, which happens exactly when $n$ is a perfect square. That lone self-paired factor is counted once, tipping the total to odd. So a whole number has an odd number of factors precisely when it is a perfect square, and the unpaired factor is the side of that square.
Priya lists every factor of 20, and Owen lists every multiple of 20. Who finishes, who never does, and why? Write the list that can be finished, and say whether 20 appears on both lists.
hints: A factor of \(20\) is a side of a rectangle with area \(20\). Can a side ever be longer than \(20\)? Now think about multiples instead. After \(20, 40, 60\), is there ever a last one?
blocks.ans: Factors of 20 are finite (1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, six of them, largest 20); multiples are infinite; 20 sits on both lists
accept: factors finite, multiples infinite, 20 on both
blocks.sol: Priya finishes, Owen never does. Every factor of \(20\) is a side of a rectangle of area \(20\), and a side can never be longer than the area allows, so no factor can climb past \(20\) itself. Hunting in pairs gives \(1\times 20\), \(2\times 10\), \(4\times 5\), and the list closes. $$1,\ 2,\ 4,\ 5,\ 10,\ 20$$ That is six factors, the largest being \(20\), and Priya is done. Owen's list has no last entry. The multiples of \(20\) are \(20, 40, 60, 80, 100, \ldots\) and from any one of them he can always take one more step of \(20\) and reach a new one. There is no biggest multiple, so he never finishes. Notice \(20\) appears on both lists at once. It is its own largest factor, since \(20=1\times 20\), and it is its own smallest positive multiple, since \(20=20\times 1\). And right at the front of Priya's list sits \(1\), a factor of \(20\) because one long row of \(20\) is a perfectly good rectangle. The finishable list is $$\boxed{1,\ 2,\ 4,\ 5,\ 10,\ 20}$$ Quick check, each pairs cleanly, \(2\times 10=20\) and \(4\times 5=20\), so nothing is missing and nothing is extra.

blocks.x: A lighthouse flashes every 8th second. How many flashes fall in the range 100 through 250 inclusive?
hints: The flashes happen exactly at the multiples of \(8\). Count every multiple of \(8\) up to \(250\), then peel off the ones that fall below \(100\).
To count multiples of \(8\) up to a number, see how many whole steps of \(8\) fit inside it. For \(250\), how many times does \(8\) go in?
blocks.sol: The flashes sit exactly at the multiples of \(8\), so this is a counting question about a stretch of the multiples line. Rather than list them all, count up to the top of the window and then subtract everything that falls below the bottom. Up to \(250\), the largest multiple of \(8\) that fits is \(248=31\times 8\), since \(32\times 8=256\) overshoots. So \(31\) multiples of \(8\) sit in \(1\) through \(250\). Below \(100\), the largest multiple of \(8\) is \(96=12\times 8\), so \(12\) multiples of \(8\) sit in \(1\) through \(99\). The flashes actually inside the window are what is left after removing the early ones. $$31-12=\boxed{19}$$ One caution worth naming, always check whether the endpoints are themselves multiples, since an endpoint that lands on a multiple has to be counted. Here neither \(100\) nor \(250\) is a multiple of \(8\), so no endpoint needs special care. Quick check, the first flash inside the window is \(104=13\times 8\) and the last is \(248=31\times 8\), and the flashes run \(13\times 8\) up through \(31\times 8\), which is \(31-13+1=19\) of them. The two counts agree.

blocks.x: Factors are finite, multiples run forever. Every factor of $n$ is at least $1$ and at most $n$, since its partner side is never smaller than $1$, so the factor list closes. But from any multiple you can always step once more and reach a fresh one, so multiples stretch without end. Three quiet facts ride along: $1$ is a factor of every $n$, every $n$ is both a factor and a multiple of itself, and $0$ is a multiple of every $n$. To count the multiples of $n$ from $1$ up to a value $V$, see how many whole $n$-steps fit, that is $V \div n$ with the remainder dropped. So count up to the top of a range and subtract the multiples below the bottom, watching whether each endpoint is itself a multiple.

Now picture two multiples of \(n\) added or subtracted. The shared factor \(n\) can be pulled out, so the result is still a multiple of \(n\). But adding a non-multiple onto a multiple always lands off the trail.
A machine dispenses and swallows tokens only in packs of 9. Starting at 0, a player receives 153, then 72 more, then returns 18. Find the final count, and explain why it must be a multiple of 9.
hints: Every amount the machine moves is a whole number of \(9\)s. Write each of \(153\), \(72\), and \(18\) as \(9\) times something, then collect the \(9\)s.
If three amounts are \(9\times17\), \(9\times8\), and \(9\times2\), what happens when you pull the \(9\) out in front of the whole calculation?
blocks.sol: The straight count is \(153+72-18=207\). Now for the part that matters. Every amount the machine touches is a whole number of \(9\)s, so write them that way, \(153=9\times17\), \(72=9\times8\), and \(18=9\times2\). The running count is then $$9\times17+9\times8-9\times2=9\times(17+8-2)=9\times23=\boxed{207}.$$ The \(9\) was a shared factor of every piece, so it slid out in front of the whole combination and left a whole number, \(23\), behind. That is the key idea. Adding or subtracting whole numbers of \(9\)s can only ever build another whole number of \(9\)s. In general, if two amounts are \(9a\) and \(9b\), then \(9a+9b=9(a+b)\) and \(9a-9b=9(a-b)\), still a whole number of \(9\)-steps. Picture it as the machine stacking full rows of \(9\). Two such blocks set on top of each other make one taller block, still full rows of \(9\) all the way up. Quick check, \(9\times23=207\), and \(153+72-18=207\) too.

blocks.x: A turnstile reads 240, a multiple of 12. A glitch adds 17. Explain why 240 + 17 cannot be a multiple of 12, then find the smallest positive number to add to restore a multiple of 12.
hints: Suppose \(240+17\) were a multiple of \(12\). Then \(17\) would be the difference of two multiples of \(12\). What does the previous problem say such a difference has to be?
The new count is \(257\). Find the next multiple of \(12\) above it, then see how far away it sits.
blocks.sol: Suppose, just to test it, that \(240+17\) were itself a multiple of \(12\). Both \(240\) and that supposed total would then be multiples of \(12\), so their difference would be a multiple of \(12\) as well, by the closure you just found. But that difference is exactly \(17\), and \(17\) is not a multiple of \(12\). The supposition collapses, so \(240+17=257\) is not a multiple of \(12\). The same reasoning works every time. A multiple plus a non-multiple is always a non-multiple. To repair the count, find the next multiple of \(12\) above \(257\). Since \(12\times21=252\) and \(12\times22=264\), the count \(257\) sits just past \(252\), and the next marker is \(264\). So you must add $$264-257=\boxed{7}.$$ Here is the idea underneath. The stray \(17\) is \(5\) past a multiple of \(12\), since \(17=12+5\). That \(5\) is all that knocked the count off the trail, so the count now sits \(5\) past a marker and needs \(12-5=7\) more to reach the next one. The remainder of the stray number alone decided the whole gap. Check, \(257+7=264=12\times22\).

blocks.x: Envelope \(A\) holds a multiple of 8 dollars, and envelope \(B\) holds a multiple of 8 dollars. Show that the two cannot total exactly 250 dollars, and find the nearest achievable total.
hints: By closure, what kind of number is \(A+B\) forced to be, whatever \(A\) and \(B\) are?
Is \(250\) a multiple of \(8\)? Compare it with \(8\times31\) and \(8\times32\).
blocks.ans: Impossible; the nearest reachable total is 248
blocks.sol: You do not need either amount. Both envelopes hold multiples of \(8\), so by closure their sum \(A+B\) is forced to be a multiple of \(8\) as well. The whole question becomes a single fact about \(250\). Is \(250\) a multiple of \(8\)? Since \(8\times31=248\) and \(8\times32=256\), the number \(250\) lands between two neighbouring multiples of \(8\) and is not one of them. So no two such envelopes can ever sum to \(250\), and the announcement is impossible. The nearest total they could actually reach is the closer of those two neighbours, $$\boxed{248},$$ which is just \(2\) below \(250\) and is reachable, for instance with \(A=240\) and \(B=8\). Notice what closure did here. It let you prove something about a sum whose two pieces you could not even see.

blocks.x: Suppose the multiples of one number coincide with the multiples of a factor it contains. A multiple of 12, is it automatically a multiple of 3? The next problem settles which direction this inheritance runs.

Multiples stay multiples, two ways. First, closure. Add or subtract two multiples of $n$ and you land on a multiple of $n$ again, since the shared $n$ factors out, $na \pm nb = n(a \pm b)$. The sharper half follows. A multiple of $n$ plus a non-multiple can never be a multiple, or else subtracting would force the stray number to be a multiple too. Second, chaining. If $a$ is a factor of $b$, then every multiple of $b$ is automatically a multiple of $a$, since the multiple carries $b$ inside it, and $b$ carries $a$. A multiple of $12$ is therefore a multiple of $1, 2, 3, 4, 6$ as well. The chain runs only downward toward smaller factors, never up, which is why a multiple of $3$ need not be a multiple of $12$.
Bundles of 12 beeswax sheets, with \(12 = 4 \times 3\). (1) Must a whole number of bundles be a multiple of 3? (2) Of 4? (3) Is every multiple of 3 a multiple of 12? Give a counterexample.
hints: For (1) and (2), picture one bundle as \(3\) rows of \(4\) (or \(4\) rows of \(3\)). If every single bundle already splits into a whole number of threes, what happens when you have several bundles stacked together?

For (3), look for a heap that you can split into groups of \(3\) with none left over, yet that is too small or too lopsided to fill whole bundles of \(12\). Try counting up \(3, 6, 9, \ldots\) and check each against \(12\).
blocks.sol: This whole problem is one idea, chaining. A multiple of a multiple is a multiple, so once \(12\) is built from \(3\) and \(4\), anything built from \(12\) inherits those smaller pieces. (1) A delivery of full bundles is some whole number of bundles, say \(n\) of them, so the sheet count is \(n\times12\). Replace each bundle by its \(3\) rows of \(4\). Every bundle becomes \(4+4+4\), a whole number of threes, so $$n\times12 = n\times(4\times3) = (n\times4)\times3,$$ which is \(3\) multiplied by a whole number. So the count is a multiple of \(3\). The threes that hid inside one bundle are still there when you stack many bundles. (2) Yes, by the same chaining, and now using the other layout. Each bundle is \(3\) rows of \(4\), so $$n\times12 = n\times(3\times4) = (n\times3)\times4,$$ which is \(4\) times a whole number. So a multiple of \(12\) is always a multiple of \(4\) too. (3) No, the arrow does not run backward. Being a multiple of \(3\) does not force a pile to be a multiple of \(12\). A pile of $$\boxed{6}$$ sheets splits cleanly into \(2\) groups of \(3\), so it is a multiple of \(3\), yet \(6\) is less than one full bundle of \(12\), so it is not a multiple of \(12\). Quick check, \(6=3\times2\) confirms the multiple of \(3\), and \(6\) sitting between \(0\) and \(12\) confirms it is not a multiple of \(12\). (The smallest multiple of \(3\) that misses \(12\) is actually \(3\) itself, so \(6\) is just one easy witness, not the smallest.) Chaining only flows one way, from the bigger building block down to the pieces inside it, never back up.

blocks.x: A secret number is a multiple of 6, a perfect square, and lies between 100 and 400 inclusive. Find every possible value.
hints: If a square \(k\times k\) is a multiple of \(6\), the side \(k\) itself must supply the factor \(2\) and the factor \(3\). What does that force \(k\) to be a multiple of?
So the secret number is the square of a multiple of \(6\). Which multiples of \(6\) have squares between \(100\) and \(400\)?
blocks.sol: The two conditions, perfect square and multiple of \(6\), squeeze together harder than they first look. Suppose the number is \(k\times k\) and is a multiple of \(6\). A multiple of \(6\) carries a factor of \(2\) and a factor of \(3\). But \(2\) and \(3\) cannot come from nowhere. For the square \(k\times k\) to contain them, the side \(k\) must already supply a factor of \(2\) and a factor of \(3\) on its own, which makes \(k\) a multiple of \(6\). Then \(k\times k\) is a multiple of \(6\times6=36\). So the hunt collapses to squaring multiples of \(6\) and keeping the ones in range. Walking them, \(6^2=36\) is below \(100\), \(12^2=144\) and \(18^2=324\) both land between \(100\) and \(400\), and \(24^2=576\) overshoots. Nothing else can appear, since any valid number had to be the square of a multiple of \(6\) and those are the only such squares in the window. The secret number is therefore $$\boxed{144 \text{ or } 324}.$$ Check, \(144=12^2\) and \(144=6\times24\), while \(324=18^2\) and \(324=6\times54\), so both are genuine perfect squares and genuine multiples of \(6\) inside the range. Notice that asking for a square that is also a multiple of \(6\) forces a multiple of \(36\).

review.x: A school fits 40 lockers into one full rectangular block. List all factor pairs of 40 and report how many factors there are in all.
hints: Climb from \(1\) and test each small number in turn. The moment one divides \(40\), the partner side is handed to you for free, since \(d\) lockers per row forces \(40\div d\) rows.

Watch the two sides of each pair close in on each other. Once the next number you would test is larger than the partner it would pair with, every block past that point is one you have already seen turned on its side, so the hunt is done.
The pairs are \(1\times40,\ 2\times20,\ 4\times10,\ 5\times8\). Read both numbers out of every pair, sort them, and count how many you have.
review.sol: Climb from \(1\) and let each small factor fix its partner, since a side of \(d\) lockers forces \(40\div d\) on the opposite edge. $$1\times 40,\qquad 2\times 20,\qquad 4\times 10,\qquad 5\times 8$$ The number \(3\) is skipped since it does not divide \(40\), and so are \(6\) and \(7\). Each small side that works instantly fixes its larger partner, so the factors come out already sorted into matched pairs whose product is \(40\). Now watch where to stop. After \(5\) the next try would be \(6\), but \(6\) would pair with a partner smaller than \(6\), one already standing inside the pair \(5\times 8\). Once the side you are about to test would be larger than the partner it pairs with, you have already met every block, just mirrored, so the search is finished. Reading both numbers out of every pair and sorting them gives $$1,\ 2,\ 4,\ 5,\ 8,\ 10,\ 20,\ 40,$$ which is \(\boxed{8}\) factors. They come in four clean pairs, no side meeting itself, so the count is even, exactly what you expect from a number that is not a perfect square. Check the middle pair, \(5\times 8 = 40\), the pile we started with. The general idea is that factors travel in partner pairs that multiply to the number, so you only hunt up to where a side would meet its own partner, and each small factor you find brings its larger partner along for free.

review.x: A ring-toss scoreboard only ever adds 8 points per throw. Five friends claim scores of 24, 30, 48, 56, and 60. How many of these could the game actually show?
hints: A score is possible exactly when \(8\) divides it with nothing left over, since the board only climbs \(8\) at a time. Try dividing each score by \(8\) and watch for a remainder.
Count the multiples of \(8\) out loud, \(8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56, 64\), and check off any claimed score you land on exactly. The ones you skip over cannot appear.
review.sol: Since the board moves up \(8\) at a time and never by anything smaller, a score can show up only if it is built from a whole number of \(8\)-point throws, which is exactly what it means to be a multiple of \(8\). So I test each score by dividing by \(8\) and asking whether it comes out clean. $$24 = 8 \times 3, \quad 48 = 8 \times 6, \quad 56 = 8 \times 7$$ Those three land squarely on a multiple of \(8\), so they are scores the game really could display, reached in \(3\), \(6\), and \(7\) successful throws. The other two miss. Dividing \(30\) by \(8\) gives \(3\) with \(6\) left over, since \(8 \times 3 = 24\) and \(8 \times 4 = 32\) overshoots, so \(30\) is stranded between two multiples and can never appear. Dividing \(60\) by \(8\) gives \(7\) with \(4\) left over, since \(8 \times 7 = 56\) and \(8 \times 8 = 64\), so \(60\) is impossible too. The possible scores are $$\boxed{24,\ 48,\ 56}$$ Check the largest one, \(8 \times 7 = 56\), seven clean throws and not a point wasted. The general idea is that a number is a multiple of \(8\) exactly when \(8\) divides it with zero remainder, so testing for a multiple is just one division and a glance at what, if anything, is left over.

review.x: Decide whether each claim about 61 is true or false. (a) 1 is a factor of 61. (b) 61 is a multiple of 61. (c) 0 is a multiple of 61. (d) 61 is a factor of 0. Enter your four verdicts in order.
hints: Lean on the definition. b is a factor of a exactly when \(a = b \times (\text{whole number})\), and then a is a multiple of b. Test each claim by trying to write that little equation.
For (a) ask what \(61 \div 1\) is, and for (b) ask what \(61 \div 61\) is. For (c) and (d), notice they are the same sentence \(0 = 61 \times 0\) read from its two ends.
review.sol: The whole question turns on one definition, that \(b\) is a factor of \(a\) when \(a = b \times (\text{whole number})\), and in the same breath \(a\) is then a multiple of \(b\). So I just hunt for that equation in each claim. For (a), \(61 = 1 \times 61\), so \(1\) is a factor of \(61\), and in fact \(1\) is a factor of every whole number since multiplying by \(1\) changes nothing. True. For (b), \(61 = 61 \times 1\), so \(61\) is a multiple of itself, just as every number is, since one copy of a number is already a multiple of it. True. Now (c) and (d) are not two separate facts at all, they are the single line $$0 = 61 \times 0$$ read from its two ends. Read with \(0\) as the result, it says \(0\) is a multiple of \(61\), so (c) is True, and indeed \(0\) is a multiple of every number, because zero copies of anything still land on \(0\). Read with \(61\) as the thing doing the multiplying, the same line says \(61\) is a factor of \(0\), so (d) is True, and every number is a factor of \(0\). All four claims hold, so the verdict is \(\boxed{\text{all four true}}\). Check the equation behind the last two, \(61 \times 0 = 0\), one sentence read two ways. The general idea is that \(1\) divides everything, every number is a multiple of itself, and \(0\) is the universal multiple that every number divides, with "factor" and "multiple" being the same relationship seen from opposite sides.

review.x: Find the smallest and largest factors of 56, and give a one-line reason for each.
accept: the smallest is 1 and the largest is 56
hints: A factor of \(56\) is any whole number that divides it evenly, so think about the two rectangles at the far ends, the single long strip and the one stacked into a single column.

One special number divides every whole number with nothing left over, which fixes the smallest factor right away. For the largest, ask whether any side length could ever be bigger than the whole bed itself.

Every factor of \(56\) travels with a partner, and the two partners multiply to \(56\). Picture the partner of the smallest whole number that divides anything, and picture what a factor bigger than \(56\) would force its partner to be. Both ends of the list fall straight out of that partner pairing once you push each side as far as it can go.
review.sol: Every factor of \(56\) travels with a partner, the two side lengths of a rectangle, and the two numbers always multiply to \(56\). Start at the bottom end. The number \(1\) divides every whole number cleanly, since \(56=1\times56\), so \(1\) is always present and nothing smaller than \(1\) is a positive whole number, which makes \(1\) the smallest factor of any number. Now climb to the top end. Suppose some factor \(f\) were larger than \(56\) itself. Its partner would be \(56\div f\), and since \(f\) is bigger than \(56\) that partner would have to be a whole number smaller than \(1\), which simply does not exist. So no factor can exceed the number, and the largest factor is the number reaching all the way up to its own size, namely \(56\), paired with \(1\) on the other side. $$\text{smallest}=1\times56,\qquad \text{largest}=56\times1$$ The two bookends are therefore $$\boxed{1\ \text{and}\ 56}.$$ Check, \(1\times56=56\) and \(56\times1=56\), both rebuild the original bed, and the same reasoning would hand back \(1\) and the number itself for any whole number you choose. The general idea is that the factors of a number are bookended by \(1\) on the low end and the number on the high end, because \(1\) divides everything and a factor can never climb past the number it divides.

review.x: Two bookends sit on every factor list. \(1\) is always the smallest factor, the single full-length row, and the number itself is always the largest, since no side of a rectangle can outrun its own area. So a factor of \(n\) is always trapped between \(1\) and \(n\), which is why a factor hunt can never wander off forever.
Which of 72, 81, and 98 has an odd number of factors? Explain through self-pairing.
hints: Every factor \(d\) of a number teams up with its partner \(n \div d\), so the factors normally march in two-by-two. A factor can lose its partner only when \(d\) and \(n \div d\) are the same number, which happens exactly when \(n\) is a perfect square.

So the lone puzzle is whichever piece count is some whole number times itself. Notice \(72\) and \(98\) both sit between two neighboring squares, so try squaring the small whole numbers in that range and see which piece count one of them matches.
review.sol: The quick way to spot an odd factor count is to remember how factors travel, since each factor \(d\) of a number \(n\) pairs with its partner \(n \div d\), and the two multiply back to \(n\). Pair off this way and the factors normally come two at a time, which makes the total even. The only escape is a factor whose partner is itself, where \(d = n \div d\), and that forces \(d \times d = n\). So a number breaks the even pattern exactly when it is a perfect square, because then its square root stands alone with no separate partner to swap with. The whole question becomes which of \(72\), \(81\), \(98\) is a whole number times itself. Squaring the nearby whole numbers gives $$8 \times 8 = 64, \qquad 9 \times 9 = 81, \qquad 10 \times 10 = 100,$$ and only \(81\) appears, sitting right at \(9 \times 9\). For \(81\) the side \(9\) pairs with itself, the lone unpaired factor that tips the count to odd, while the rest still pair up as \(1 \times 81\) and \(3 \times 27\). The number \(72\) falls between \(64\) and \(81\) and \(98\) falls between \(81\) and \(100\), so neither is any whole number squared, which means every one of their factors finds a distinct partner and their counts stay even. The puzzle with an odd number of factors is \(\boxed{81}\). Check that \(81\) really is special by listing its factors, \(1, 3, 9, 27, 81\), which is five of them, an odd count, with \(9\) standing in the middle as its own partner. The general idea is that an odd factor count is the fingerprint of a perfect square, since only a perfect square owns a factor that pairs with itself.

review.x: A ribbon is marked every 23 centimeters. What is the largest multiple of 23 below 500?
hints: The cut marks land on \(23, 46, 69,\) and so on, the multiples of \(23\). The biggest one under \(500\) is just as many whole \(23\)-steps as fit beneath the cap.
Divide \(500\) by \(23\) and throw away the remainder. That whole number of steps, times \(23\), is the mark you want.
Since \(500 \div 23\) is \(21\) with something left over, take \(21\) full steps and compute \(21 \times 23\).
review.sol: The largest multiple of \(23\) below a cap is found by fitting as many whole \(23\)-steps under the cap as I can, so I divide and round down. $$500 \div 23 = 21 \text{ remainder } 17$$ The remainder \(17\) tells me \(500\) is not itself a multiple of \(23\), and that exactly \(21\) full steps fit beneath it. Those \(21\) steps land the cut at $$21 \times 23 = 483.$$ The next mark would be \(22 \times 23 = 506\), which sails past \(500\), so there is no multiple of \(23\) hiding between \(483\) and \(500\). The tailor cuts at \(\boxed{483}\) centimeters. Check, \(483\) is under \(500\) and \(483 + 23 = 506\) is over, so \(483\) really is the last mark before the cap. The general idea is that the greatest multiple of \(n\) below a bound is the bound divided by \(n\) with the remainder dropped, then multiplied back by \(n\).

review.x: A bakery packs rolls in trays of 26. What is the smallest three-digit multiple of 26?
hints: A three-digit number is anything from 100 up to 999, so you want the first multiple of 26 that lands at or above 100.
Instead of guessing, divide 100 by 26 to see how many trays it takes to get near the bound, then round that count up to a whole number of trays.
\(100 \div 26 \approx 3.8\), and you cannot bake a fraction of a tray, so round up to 4 trays and compute \(26 \times 4\).
review.sol: Climb the multiples of 26 one tray at a time and watch for the moment the total first reaches three digits. $$26\times 1 = 26,\qquad 26\times 2 = 52,\qquad 26\times 3 = 78,\qquad 26\times 4 = 104$$ Three trays give \(78\), which is still two digits, so it falls short of the \(100\) mark. The next tray jumps to \(104\), the first total that reaches three digits, so the answer is \(\boxed{104}\). Here is the quicker way to see it without listing, which is the real idea. A three-digit number is anything from \(100\) upward, so I ask how many trays it takes to reach \(100\) by dividing, \(100 \div 26 \approx 3.8\). Since I cannot bake \(3.8\) trays, I round that count up to the next whole tray, \(4\), and then \(26 \times 4 = 104\). Rounding up is the key move, because rounding down to \(3\) would land below the bound at \(78\). Check the answer is genuinely a multiple of 26 by confirming \(26 \times 4 = 104\), and that \(104 \div 26 = 4\) with nothing left over. The general idea is that the smallest multiple of \(n\) at or above a bound \(B\) comes from dividing \(B\) by \(n\) and rounding the quotient up to a whole number, then multiplying back by \(n\).

review.x: Two-digit codes lit from inside are multiples of 6 whose digits sum to 6. How many codes light up?
hints: Start from the digit rule rather than the multiples. A two-digit code has a tens digit and a units digit, and you need those two digits to add to \(6\). Write down every pair of digits that sums to \(6\) first, then worry about the multiple of \(6\) part.

The tens digit can run from \(1\) to \(6\), and once you pick it the units digit is forced so the two add to \(6\). That gives the codes \(15, 24, 33, 42, 51, 60\). Now you only need to decide which of these six are multiples of \(6\).

A multiple of \(6\) is just a number that is both even and a multiple of \(3\). Every code on your short list already has digit sum \(6\), so each one is automatically a multiple of \(3\). That leaves a single test, which of \(15, 24, 33, 42, 51, 60\) are even.
review.sol: The better move is to build the codes from the digit rule first, since the condition that the two digits add to \(6\) is far more restrictive than the multiple of \(6\) condition and leaves only a tiny list to check. A two-digit code has a tens digit and a units digit, and the tens digit cannot be \(0\), so let the tens digit run from \(1\) up to \(6\). Each choice forces the units digit, because the two digits must total \(6\), which gives the pairs \(1\) and \(5\), then \(2\) and \(4\), then \(3\) and \(3\), then \(4\) and \(2\), then \(5\) and \(1\), then \(6\) and \(0\). A tens digit of \(7\) or more is impossible since even a units digit of \(0\) would already push the sum past \(6\), so the complete list of two-digit numbers with digit sum \(6\) is $$15,\ 24,\ 33,\ 42,\ 51,\ 60,$$ exactly six of them and no more. Now bring in the multiple of \(6\) requirement, and here is the shortcut. A number is a multiple of \(6\) precisely when it is both even and a multiple of \(3\), and every code on this short list has digit sum \(6\), which is itself a multiple of \(3\), so every one of these six is already divisible by \(3\) for free. The only gate left is being even, so I just keep the codes that end in an even digit, which are \(24\), \(42\), and \(60\), while \(15\), \(33\), and \(51\) end in odd digits and fall away. The lit floats therefore carry the codes $$\boxed{24,\ 42,\ 60}.$$ Check each one, \(24=6\times 4\) with \(2+4=6\), \(42=6\times 7\) with \(4+2=6\), and \(60=6\times 10\) with \(6+0=6\), all three passing both rules, and since I built the candidate list from every possible digit pair that sums to \(6\), nothing was skipped and no fourth code can exist. The general idea is that when two rules overlap, you start from whichever rule produces the shorter list, and a digit sum being fixed at \(6\) handily settles the divisibility by \(3\) on its own, so the search collapses to a single even or odd check.

review.x: Find the largest three-digit multiple of 15 whose three digits are all different.
hints: A number is a multiple of 15 exactly when it is a multiple of both 3 and 5, so it has to end in 0 or 5. That alone narrows the search a lot.
Start at the top three-digit multiple of 15 and step downward by 15, checking each one for a repeated digit.
The top multiple is 990, but its two 9s break the all-different rule. Keep dropping by 15 and look at the next candidate.
review.sol: Since \(15 = 3 \times 5\), a multiple of 15 must be divisible by both 3 and 5, so it has to end in \(0\) or \(5\). The largest three-digit multiple of 15 sits right below 1000, and \(1000 \div 15\) is not a whole number, so I climb down to the nearest one. $$990 = 15 \times 66$$ That is the greatest three-digit multiple of 15, but its digits are \(9, 9, 0\), and the two 9s repeat, so it fails the all-different rule. Stepping down by 15 lands me on the next candidate. $$975 = 15 \times 65$$ Its digits are \(9, 7, 5\), all three different from one another, so this one qualifies. Because I am walking downward from the top and 975 is the first survivor I meet, nothing larger can work, and the answer is \(\boxed{975}\). Check that 975 really is a multiple of 15 by testing both factors, its digit sum is \(9 + 7 + 5 = 21\) which is divisible by 3, and it ends in 5 so it is divisible by 5, so it is divisible by 15. The general idea is that you can hunt for the best number meeting a rule by searching from the extreme end and walking inward, stopping the instant the extra constraint, here all digits distinct, is finally satisfied.

review.x: An amphitheater has 7 rows with 14, 28, 42, … seats. Find the total number of seats.
hints: Each row count is \(14\) times its position from the front, so row one holds \(14\times 1\), row two holds \(14\times 2\), and so on up to row seven.
Once every row is written as \(14\) times something, you can pull the \(14\) out front and only add \(1+2+3+4+5+6+7\).
Add \(1\) through \(7\) by pairing \(1\) with \(7\), \(2\) with \(6\), \(3\) with \(5\), three pairs of \(8\) with \(4\) left in the middle, which gives \(28\). Then multiply by \(14\).
review.sol: Start by writing down what sits in each row as you climb. The front row has \(14\), the next has \(28\), then \(42\), and since every row adds another \(14\), the seven counts are \(14, 28, 42, 56, 70, 84, 98\). The slow way is to add those seven biggish numbers straight across, but there is a friendlier move, since every one of them is \(14\) times its row position counting from the front, $$14 = 14\times 1,\quad 28 = 14\times 2,\quad 42 = 14\times 3,\quad \dots,\quad 98 = 14\times 7.$$ Because the same factor of \(14\) rides along with every term, I can pull it out front and rewrite the whole total as \(14\times(1+2+3+4+5+6+7)\). Now the hard part has shrunk to adding the plain counting numbers \(1\) through \(7\), and the pairing trick finishes it quickly, since \(1+7=8\), \(2+6=8\), and \(3+5=8\), which is three pairs of \(8\) with the lonely \(4\) sitting in the middle, so \(3\times 8 + 4 = 28\). That leaves a single clean multiplication, $$14\times 28 = \boxed{392}.$$ Check by grouping the original row counts the same friendly way, \(14+98 = 112\), \(28+84 = 112\), \(42+70 = 112\), three pairs of \(112\) plus the middle row of \(56\), and \(3\times 112 + 56 = 336 + 56 = 392\), which matches. The general idea is that when a list of quantities all share a common step, you can factor that step out front and only sum the small counting numbers that remain, often pairing them from the ends to land the answer in your head.

review.x: A runner claps at every 13th metre. How many claps fall strictly between metres 150 and 850?
hints: The claps land exactly at the multiples of \(13\). Instead of listing them, count every multiple of \(13\) up to the top of the stretch, then peel off the ones that sit below the bottom.
To count multiples of \(13\) below a bound, see how many whole \(13\)-steps fit inside it. How many times does \(13\) go into \(849\), and how many times into \(150\)?

First multiple of \(13\) above \(150\) is \(13\times 12 = 156\), since \(13\times 11 = 143\) is too low. Last one below \(850\) is \(13\times 65 = 845\), since \(13\times 66 = 858\) overshoots. Now count from the \(12\)th marker through the \(65\)th.
review.sol: The claps sit exactly at the multiples of \(13\), so this is a counting question about a stretch of the multiples line. Rather than list every post, count up to the top of the window and then subtract everything that falls below the bottom. For the top, the largest multiple of \(13\) that stays strictly under \(850\) is \(845 = 13\times 65\), since \(13\times 66 = 858\) sails past \(850\). So \(65\) multiples of \(13\) sit from the start of the line up through \(845\). For the bottom, the largest multiple of \(13\) that stays at or below \(150\) is \(143 = 13\times 11\), since \(13\times 12 = 156\) already pokes above \(150\). So \(11\) multiples of \(13\) sit below the window. The claps actually inside the stretch are what is left after removing those early ones. $$65 - 11 = \boxed{54}$$ One caution always worth naming, check whether either endpoint is itself a multiple, since an endpoint landing on a marker would have to be counted. Here \(150 = 13\times 11 + 7\) and \(850 = 13\times 65 + 5\), so neither \(150\) nor \(850\) is a multiple of \(13\), and the word strictly costs us nothing. Quick check, the first clap inside the window is \(156 = 13\times 12\) and the last is \(845 = 13\times 65\), and the markers run from the \(12\)th through the \(65\)th, which is \(65 - 12 + 1 = 54\) of them. Both counts agree. The general idea is that to count the multiples of \(n\) inside a range you count how many sit up to the top and subtract how many sit below the bottom, keeping one eye on whether each endpoint is itself a multiple.

review.x: Two trucks each carry a multiple of 15 apples. Show 400 is impossible, then find the nearest reachable total.
hints: Each truck holds a multiple of \(15\). What can you say about the sum of two multiples of \(15\)? Then ask whether \(400\) is itself a multiple of \(15\).

Closure says the sum of two multiples of \(15\) is again a multiple of \(15\). Since \(15\times 26=390\) and \(15\times 27=405\), the number \(400\) sits strictly between two neighboring multiples, so it is not one of them.

The only totals possible are multiples of \(15\). Compare the two multiples on either side of \(400\), namely \(390\) and \(405\), and see which is closer, then check that the closer one is actually reachable as a sum of two multiples of \(15\).
review.sol: Write each truck as a multiple of \(15\), say \(15a\) apples on one and \(15b\) on the other, where \(a\) and \(b\) are whole numbers. Their total factors a \(15\) right out front. $$15a+15b=15(a+b)$$ Since \(a+b\) is again a whole number, the total is itself a multiple of \(15\). This is closure, adding two multiples of \(15\) can never escape the multiples of \(15\). So the only question is whether \(400\) is one of them. Walk the multiples of \(15\) near \(400\). $$15\times 26=390,\qquad 15\times 27=405$$ The number \(400\) lands strictly between these two neighbors, with no multiple of \(15\) in the gap, so \(400\) is not a multiple of \(15\) and the dispatcher's claim is impossible. Now hunt for the reachable total nearest \(400\). The candidates are exactly its two neighbors, \(390\) and \(405\). Measure each gap. $$400-390=10,\qquad 405-400=5$$ So \(405\) wins, sitting only \(5\) away while \(390\) is twice as far. It remains to confirm \(405\) is genuinely reachable as a sum of two trucks, each a multiple of \(15\). It is, for instance one truck with a single crate and the other with \(26\) crates. $$15+390=405$$ So the total cannot be \(400\), and the nearest total two such trucks could actually carry is \(\boxed{405}\). Check, \(405=15\times 27\), a clean multiple of \(15\), and \(15+390\) builds it from two valid trucks. The general idea is that a closed set, here the multiples of \(15\), can prove a target is out of reach the moment the target fails the membership test, and the best you can do is the nearest member to it.

review.x: A bead artist works only in kits of 24. For each of 2, 3, 4, 8, 9, and 16, decide whether the bead count must be a multiple of that number. How many of the six are guaranteed?
hints: A multiple of \(24\) can be written as \(24 \times k\) for some whole number \(k\). First list everything that divides \(24\) itself, since each of those factors rides along inside every copy of \(24\).

If \(d\) is a factor of \(24\), then \(24 = d \times m\), so \(24 \times k = d \times (mk)\), which means \(d\) divides the count too. Check whether \(9\) and \(16\) are actually factors of \(24\) before you trust them.
For any size that is not a factor of \(24\), the number \(24\) itself is the cleanest test case, since \(24\) is certainly a multiple of \(24\). Does \(9\) divide \(24\)? Does \(16\) divide \(24\)?
review.sol: The whole game is to see which numbers are baked into \(24\), because a multiple of \(24\) carries along every factor that \(24\) already has, and nothing more is promised. Start by laying out the factors of \(24\) in pairs, $$1\times 24,\qquad 2\times 12,\qquad 3\times 8,\qquad 4\times 6,$$ so the factors of \(24\) are \(1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 6,\ 8,\ 12,\ 24\). Here is the chaining idea. The bead count is some \(24\times k\). If \(d\) is a factor of \(24\), then \(24 = d\times m\) for a whole number \(m\), so the count is \(24\times k = d\times(mk)\), and that shows \(d\) divides the count. In words, whatever divides \(24\) divides every multiple of \(24\), and the inheritance only flows downward to \(24\)'s own factors. Run the six sizes through this test. Since \(2,\ 3,\ 4,\) and \(8\) are all on the factor list of \(24\), the count must be a multiple of each of them. But \(9\) is not a factor of \(24\), because \(24 = 9\times 2 + 6\) leaves a remainder of \(6\), and \(16\) is not a factor either, since \(24 = 16 + 8\) leaves a remainder of \(8\). So neither \(9\) nor \(16\) is guaranteed, and the cleanest counterexample for both is \(24\) itself, which is a multiple of \(24\) yet is divisible by neither \(9\) nor \(16\). Collecting the guaranteed sizes gives $$\boxed{2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 8}.$$ Check the trickiest survivor, \(8\), on a real multiple, \(24\times 5 = 120 = 8\times 15\), divisible just as promised, while \(120\) is not a multiple of \(16\) since \(120 = 16\times 7 + 8\). The general idea is that being a multiple of \(n\) only forces divisibility by the factors of \(n\), so divisibility chains downward through \(n\)'s factor list and never reaches a number that is not a factor of \(n\).

review.x: Find every perfect square that is a multiple of 45 and stays below 2000. How many such numbers are there?
hints: A multiple of 45 must carry the factors hiding inside 45. Break 45 into primes and ask what the number is forced to contain.
Write the square as \(s \times s\). If the product \(s\times s\) must contain a single factor of 5 and a factor of 9, what is the side \(s\) forced to contain?
Once you see that \(s\) must be a multiple of 15, only check \(15^2\), \(30^2\), \(45^2\), and stop the moment you pass 2000.
review.sol: First look at what "multiple of 45" really requires. Since \(45 = 9 \times 5 = 3^2 \times 5\), every multiple of 45 must carry at least two factors of 3 and one factor of 5. Now suppose the code is a perfect square, so I can write it as \(s \times s\) for some whole number \(s\). The key step is to track each prime through that product. The square needs a factor of 5, but 5 is a prime, so the two copies of \(s\) cannot share it loosely. For \(s\times s\) to contain a 5 at all, one of the copies of \(s\) must contain a 5, and then the other copy contains one too, so the square actually carries \(5\times 5\). The same pressure works for 3. The square needs a factor of 3, and since one copy of \(s\) must supply it, both copies do, so the square carries \(3 \times 3\) just from a single 3 living in \(s\). Putting those together, \(s\) must contain a 3 and a 5, which means \(s\) is a multiple of \(3 \times 5 = 15\), and therefore the square is a multiple of \(15^2 = 225\). That is the key, the two separate demands for a 9 and a 5 combine and force the code to be a multiple of 225, not merely of 45. Now I only have to walk through the multiples of 15 and square them, watching the 2000 ceiling. $$15^2 = 225,\qquad 30^2 = 900,\qquad 45^2 = 2025$$ The first two sit safely below 2000, but \(45^2 = 2025\) overshoots, and every later square is larger still, so the search is complete. Nothing was left out, because any valid code had to be \(15^2\) times a perfect square, and \(225 \times 1\) and \(225 \times 4\) are the only such values under 2000. The codes are \(\boxed{225 \text{ and } 900}\). Check that each truly fits, since \(225 = 15^2\) is a square and \(225 = 45 \times 5\) is a multiple of 45, while \(900 = 30^2\) is a square and \(900 = 45 \times 20\) is a multiple of 45. The general idea is that a perfect square must contain every prime to an even power, so demanding a square that is a multiple of a number forces the square to be a multiple of that number's "squared-up" version, here 45 rounding up to 225.

review.x: A perfect number equals the sum of its proper factors: \(1+2+3=6\) and \(1+2+4+7+14=28\). They are rare, and no one has ever found an odd perfect number or proved none exists.

blocks.x: Place value hides a shortcut. Given a big number like 6,351, must you divide to know whether 3 divides it, or can you glance and know? It comes from place value.
Five claim tags have numbers 2,470; 3,805; 6,118; 9,640; and 7,333. Without dividing, decide which are multiples of 5. Which single digit settles the question? How many of the five tags win a prize?
hints: You do not have to divide the whole tag by 5. Write one tag as a stack of tens plus its leftover ones digit, like 2,470 = 247 tens and 0 ones, and watch which part you can stop worrying about.

A whole number of tens is already a multiple of 5, since every ten is 5 twice. By closure from the last lesson, throwing away a multiple of 5 leaves the question resting on the single ones digit. Which ones digits make the leftover a multiple of 5?
Sort the tags by their last digit alone. A tag wins for 5 when it ends in 0 or 5, and it also wins for 10 only when it ends in 0.
blocks.sol: Take any tag and cut it right after the tens, so the ones digit stands alone. Every tag then reads as a whole number of tens plus that single leftover, $$N = 10k + u,$$ where \(k\) is everything above the ones place and \(u\) is the ones digit itself. The first piece, \(10k\), is a whole number of tens, and since one ten is \(5\times 2\), that piece is always a multiple of \(5\) no matter how large \(k\) is. By closure, adding or removing a multiple of \(5\) cannot change whether the total is a multiple of \(5\), so the prize question slides entirely onto the leftover \(u\). A tag wins for \(5\) exactly when its ones digit is \(0\) or \(5\). Reading just the last digits, \(2{,}470\) ends in \(0\), \(3{,}805\) ends in \(5\), \(6{,}118\) ends in \(8\), \(9{,}640\) ends in \(0\), and \(7{,}333\) ends in \(3\). The winners are \(\boxed{2{,}470,\ 3{,}805,\ 9{,}640}\). The same split tells the \(10\) story for free, because \(10k\) is already a clean stack of tens, so a tag is a multiple of \(10\) only when the leftover \(u\) is \(0\), which singles out \(2{,}470\) and \(9{,}640\). Check \(3{,}805 = 5\times 761\), a genuine multiple of \(5\) but not of \(10\), just as its final \(5\) promised. The general idea is that the ones digit carries the whole verdict for \(5\) and \(10\), since everything above it is a pile of tens that closure lets us ignore.

blocks.x: Band sections have 84,317; 2,000,006; 55,550; and 9,999 players. Which counts are even and can line up in perfect pairs? How many of the four can pair up perfectly?
hints: Pairing up two to a row works exactly when the count is even, so the real question is which of these four counts is even. Look hard at the end of each number before you reach for anything bigger.

Try writing a number as its tens part plus its last digit, like \(55{,}550 = 55{,}55 \times 10 + 0\) or \(84{,}317 = 8{,}431 \times 10 + 7\). Any whole number of tens is already even, so the only thing still in doubt is that final leftover digit.
For \(2{,}000{,}006\), all those zeros sit in the tens part and are already even on their own. Only the final \(6\) is left to decide the whole question, and \(6\) is even.
blocks.sol: Lining up two to a row with nobody alone is exactly asking whether each count is even, so I want to test evenness without wrestling those giant numbers. The idea is to break each count at the spot where everything to the left becomes a clean number of tens. Write any whole number as its tens part plus its single units digit, \(N = 10k + u\), where \(u\) is that last digit. The piece \(10k\) is ten times a whole number, and ten is even, so \(10k\) is even no matter how enormous \(k\) is. An even pile already splits perfectly into pairs by itself. That means the entire fate of \(N\) rests on the leftover \(u\), since adding an even chunk cannot change whether the total is even or odd. So \(N\) is even exactly when its units digit \(u\) is even. $$N = 10k + u, \qquad 10k \text{ is always even, so even-ness rides on } u$$ Now sweep the four bands, glancing only at the last digit. Brass \(84{,}317\) ends in \(7\), odd, so one player is always left without a partner. Percussion \(2{,}000{,}006\) ends in \(6\), and notice the whole \(2{,}000{,}000\) in front is a flat number of tens, already even on its own, so every one of those zeros drops out and only the final \(6\) matters, which is even, so it pairs. Woodwinds \(55{,}550\) ends in \(0\), and \(0\) is even, so it pairs cleanly too. Color guard \(9{,}999\) ends in \(9\), odd, so it cannot. The counts that line up in perfect pairs are $$\boxed{2{,}000{,}006 \text{ and } 55{,}550}.$$ Check, \(55{,}550 = 27{,}775 \times 2\) and \(2{,}000{,}006 = 1{,}000{,}003 \times 2\), both genuine doubles, while \(84{,}317\) and \(9{,}999\) each leave a remainder of \(1\) when you try. The general idea is that the tens part of any number is already a multiple of \(2\), so evenness is decided by the units digit alone, and all the other digits, however many millions they stretch across, simply do not matter.

blocks.x: The last digit settled 2, 5, and 10 because every higher place is a multiple of ten. Now try 4. Ten is not a multiple of 4, so one digit is not enough. We need to cut earlier and keep a bigger tail. Where should the cut go?
A dealer splits stacks of 5,328; 7,914; 13,500; and 60,742 cards into complete hands of 4. Write each as hundreds plus its last two digits. How many stacks deal out evenly?
hints: Try splitting each deck at the hundreds, like \(7{,}914 = 79\times100 + 14\). One hundred is \(25\) full hands of \(4\), so every full hundred already deals out evenly. That means the hundreds part never causes trouble, no matter how big it is.

If the hundreds part always deals out evenly, then whether the whole stack works comes down to that small leftover number in the last two digits. So only check whether \(28\), \(14\), \(00\), and \(42\) each split into hands of \(4\).
blocks.sol: The efficient move is to cut each deck at the hundreds, since a hundred works nicely here. One hundred is a whole number of hands, because $$100 = 4\times25,$$ so every full hundred cards deals out into exactly \(25\) complete hands of \(4\) with nothing left over. Now peel any stack apart at its last two digits, writing it as a pile of full hundreds plus the leftover. Take the first deck, \(5{,}328 = 53\times100 + 28\). The \(53\times100\) part is \(53\) hundreds, and each hundred is already a multiple of \(4\), so that whole chunk deals out evenly on its own. By closure, adding or removing such a clean chunk cannot change whether the total works, so the entire stack deals out evenly exactly when the leftover \(28\) does. That is the point, everything above the last two digits is full hundreds and can be ignored, so only the last two digits decide it. Run the four decks through this lens. $$5{,}328 = 53\times100 + 28,\qquad 7{,}914 = 79\times100 + 14$$ $$13{,}500 = 135\times100 + 00,\qquad 60{,}742 = 607\times100 + 42$$ The hundreds chunk is harmless every time, so check only the leftover. For \(5{,}328\) the leftover \(28 = 4\times7\) deals out, so it works. For \(7{,}914\) the leftover \(14\) is not a multiple of \(4\), since \(4\times3 = 12\) and \(4\times4 = 16\) skip right over it, so it fails. For \(13{,}500\) the leftover is \(00\), an empty leftover that needs no extra hand at all, so it works. For \(60{,}742\) the leftover \(42\) lands between \(40\) and \(44\), so it fails. The decks that deal out into full hands of \(4\) are $$\boxed{5{,}328 \text{ and } 13{,}500}.$$ Check the close one, \(5{,}328 = 4\times1332\), a tidy \(1332\) hands. The general idea is that since \(100\) is a multiple of \(4\), divisibility by \(4\) is decided entirely by the last two digits, because every higher place is a full bundle of hundreds that closure lets you throw away.

blocks.x: Arcade machines show totals 47,216; 9,360; and 128,500. Test only the last three digits of each for divisibility by 8. How many are divisible by 8?
hints: A whole number of strips means the total is a multiple of \(8\). Look at \(1000\). Can you write \(1000\) itself as a whole number times \(8\)? If every full thousand is already a stack of strips, then the thousands part can be thrown away and only what is left below a thousand can decide things.

Split each total right after the last three digits, like \(47{,}216 = 47\times 1000 + 216\). Closure lets you drop the \(47\times 1000\) chunk because it is a pile of complete strips, so only the three-digit tail, \(216\), is left to test against \(8\).

Now line the tests up. For \(2\) you read \(1\) digit, for \(4\) you read \(2\) digits, and for \(8\) you read \(3\). Ask which power of ten each of \(2, 4, 8\) first divides into cleanly, and the digit counts will line up by themselves.
blocks.sol: Dividing each whole total by \(8\) is the slow road, so cut the number where one chunk turns into complete strips. The key fact is that one thousand is already a stack of strips, since $$1000 = 8\times 125.$$ That means every full thousand tokens is a whole number of \(8\)-strips with nothing spare. Split each total right after its last three digits and watch the big chunk fall away by closure, because subtracting a pile of complete strips cannot change whether what remains is a whole number of strips. For the first machine, \(47{,}216 = 47\times 1000 + 216\), and the \(47\times 1000\) is all complete strips, so only \(216\) is left, and \(216 = 8\times 27\), a clean fit, so \(47{,}216\) works. For the second, \(9{,}360 = 9\times 1000 + 360\), and the tail \(360 = 8\times 45\) fits too, so \(9{,}360\) works. For the third, \(128{,}500 = 128\times 1000 + 500\), and the tail \(500\) lands between \(8\times 62 = 496\) and \(8\times 64 = 512\), so it is not a multiple of \(8\) and \(128{,}500\) fails. The totals made of whole strips are \(\boxed{47{,}216 \text{ and } 9{,}360}\). Now the reason it took three digits here when \(4\) needed only two. You read exactly as many digits as the first power of ten your divisor divides into cleanly, because that power of ten is where the chunk above it becomes throwaway. Watch the powers of two march up the powers of ten, $$2 \mid 10, \qquad 4 \mid 100, \qquad 8 \mid 1000,$$ so \(2\) is decided by the last \(1\) digit, \(4\) by the last \(2\), and \(8\) by the last \(3\). Check, the winning tails really do fit, \(8\times 27 = 216\) and \(8\times 45 = 360\). The general idea is that you slice a number at the first place value the divisor swallows whole, let closure discard everything above the slice, and judge the whole number by the small tail that remains.

blocks.x: Cut a number at the right spot and one chunk becomes a clean multiple of the divisor, so closure drops it and only a short tail decides. Split off the units digit, $N=10k+u$. Since $10$ carries a $2$ and a $5$, the whole $10k$ is a multiple of $2$, $5$, and $10$, so the verdict rides on $u$ alone. Divisible by $10$ when $u=0$, by $5$ when $u=0$ or $5$, by $2$ when $u$ is even. The same trick climbs a ladder, because each power of two divides the matching power of ten: $2\mid 10$, $4\mid 100$, $8\mid 1000$. So read the last $1$ digit for $2$, the last $2$ digits for $4$ (since $100=4\times 25$), and the last $3$ digits for $8$ (since $1000=8\times 125$).

Try the same tail trick on 9. For that, we'd need a power of ten that 9 divides, but \(10=9+1\), \(100=99+1\), \(1000=999+1\). Every power of ten misses being a multiple of 9 by exactly 1. That uniform miss is not a dead end, it is a clue.
The stamp number is 5,742. Break it by place value, write each power of ten as a multiple of 9 plus 1, and let the multiples fall away. What leftover decides whether 9 divides 5,742?
hints: Do not divide the whole 5,742 at once. Split it by place value, \(5742 = 5\times 1000 + 7\times 100 + 4\times 10 + 2\), and look at each power of ten on its own.
Write each power of ten as a multiple of 9 plus 1, so \(1000 = 999 + 1\), \(100 = 99 + 1\), \(10 = 9 + 1\). Every '\(+1\)' hands its digit straight back to you.
After the \(999\), \(99\), and \(9\) chunks fall away as multiples of 9, the only piece left to check is \(5 + 7 + 4 + 2\).
blocks.sol: The idea is to stop dividing 9 into the whole stamp number and divide it into each place-value piece instead. Spread \(5742\) out by place value, then write every power of ten as a multiple of 9 plus a leftover 1, since \(10 = 9+1\), \(100 = 99+1\), and \(1000 = 999+1\). $$5742 = 5(999+1) + 7(99+1) + 4(9+1) + 2$$ Now split each product into its big round part and its tiny leftover and gather them into two groups. $$5742 = \big(5\times 999 + 7\times 99 + 4\times 9\big) + \big(5 + 7 + 4 + 2\big)$$ The first group is built entirely from \(999\), \(99\), and \(9\), so it is a sum of multiples of 9, and by closure adding multiples of 9 keeps you on a multiple of 9. That whole chunk leaves no remainder, so it cannot change whether 9 divides the number. Everything now rides on the second group, which is exactly the digit sum \(5 + 7 + 4 + 2 = \boxed{18}\). Since \(18 = 9 \times 2\) is itself a multiple of 9, the stamp number \(5742\) is divisible by 9 too. Check by the brute road, \(5742 \div 9 = 638\) exactly, no remainder, just as the digit sum promised. The general idea is that each power of ten sits one step past a string of 9s, so the round part always vanishes as a multiple of 9 and a number leaves the same remainder on division by 9 as its digit sum does.
