blocks.cap: The rule becomes a picture for \(72 = 2^3 \times 3^2\). Each divisor picks one power of \(2\) for its column, from \(2^0\) up to \(2^3\), giving four columns, and one power of \(3\) for its row, from \(3^0\) up to \(3^2\), giving three rows. Every cell of the grid is one divisor, the product of its row and column headers, so the divisors fill a tidy \(4 \times 3\) rectangle. That is why the count is \((3+1)(2+1) = 12\), the same as adding one to each exponent and multiplying. The two green cells, \(9\) and \(36\), are the divisors that happen to be perfect squares themselves, a hint of something we will return to in a moment.

blocks.x: The fingerprint of 360 is \(360 = 2^3 \times 3^2 \times 5\). Use the add-one-and-multiply rule to count how many positive divisors 360 has without listing them.
hints: Each prime contributes one more choice than its exponent. The prime \(2\) has exponent \(3\), so it offers \(3 + 1\) choices. Do the same for \(3\) and for \(5\), then multiply.
The choice counts are \(3+1 = 4\) for the \(2\)s, \(2+1 = 3\) for the \(3\)s, and \(1+1 = 2\) for the \(5\). Multiply \(4 \times 3 \times 2\).
blocks.sol: The rule extends to any number of primes without change. For \(360 = 2^3 \times 3^2 \times 5^1\), each prime offers one more choice than its exponent. The \(2\)s give \(3 + 1 = 4\) choices, the \(3\)s give \(2 + 1 = 3\), and the single \(5\) gives \(1 + 1 = 2\). Multiplying the independent choices, $$(3+1)(2+1)(1+1) = 4 \times 3 \times 2 = \boxed{24}.$$ So \(360\) has \(24\) positive divisors, and we found that without writing even one of them down. Quick check, the smallest few divisors \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10\) already pile up fast, and the full list does run to exactly \(24\) entries. The general idea is that the add-one-and-multiply rule scales to as many primes as a number carries, turning a long listing chore into one quick multiplication.

blocks.x: Compare \(96 = 2^5 \times 3\) and \(100 = 2^2 \times 5^2\). Apply the counting rule to each. Which number has more divisors?
hints: Apply add-one-and-multiply to each. For \(96 = 2^5 \times 3\), the counts are \(5+1\) and \(1+1\). For \(100 = 2^2 \times 5^2\), the counts are \(2+1\) and \(2+1\).
\(96\) gives \(6 \times 2 = 12\) divisors, while \(100\) gives \(3 \times 3 = 9\). Which is larger?
blocks.sol: Run the rule on each fingerprint. For \(96 = 2^5 \times 3\), the divisor count is $$(5+1)(1+1) = 6 \times 2 = 12.$$ For \(100 = 2^2 \times 5^2\), it is $$(2+1)(2+1) = 3 \times 3 = 9.$$ So \(96\) has \(12\) divisors while the larger \(100\) has only \(9\), and the winner is \(\boxed{96}\). The reason is in the shape of the fingerprints. A tall stack of one prime, like the five \(2\)s in \(96\), spreads into many divisors, while \(100\) spends its size on two medium stacks that multiply to a smaller count. Quick check, \(100\) really does have just nine divisors, \(1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 50, 100\), three short of \(96\)'s dozen. The general idea is that the number of divisors depends on the shape of the prime fingerprint, the exponents, not on how big the number is.

blocks.x: The divisor count \((a+1)(b+1)\cdots\) is odd only when every exponent is even, the fingerprint of a perfect square. How many numbers from 1 to 50 have an odd number of divisors?
hints: The divisor count is a product \((a+1)(b+1)\cdots\). A product of whole numbers is odd only when every single factor in it is odd. So ask, when is every \(a+1\) odd?
Every \(a+1\) is odd exactly when every exponent \(a\) is even, which is precisely when the number is a perfect square. So count the perfect squares from \(1\) to \(50\).
blocks.sol: The divisor count is the product \((a+1)(b+1)\cdots\), and a product of whole numbers comes out odd only when every factor in it is odd. Each factor \(a+1\) is odd exactly when the exponent \(a\) is even. So a number has an odd divisor count precisely when every exponent in its fingerprint is even, and that is exactly the fingerprint of a perfect square, the same fact lesson 3.1 discovered through self-pairing. So the question becomes how many perfect squares lie from \(1\) to \(50\). They are $$1,\ 4,\ 9,\ 16,\ 25,\ 36,\ 49,$$ the squares of \(1\) through \(7\), since \(8^2 = 64\) overshoots. That is \(\boxed{7}\) numbers. Quick check, \(36 = 2^2 \times 3^2\) has \((2+1)(2+1) = 9\) divisors, an odd count, just as promised. The general idea is that the counting rule re-proves the old result, since an odd divisor count forces every exponent even, which is the fingerprint of a perfect square.

blocks.x: A number has an odd number of divisors exactly when it is a perfect square. From 3.1, only a perfect square has a factor that pairs with itself, leaving one unpaired. From the counting rule, the product $(a+1)(b+1)\cdots$ is odd only when every exponent is even, which is the fingerprint of a perfect square. Two stories, one conclusion.
Find the smallest whole number with exactly 12 divisors by breaking 12 into exponent-plus-one pieces, building the smallest number from each, and comparing.
hints: You need \((a+1)(b+1)\cdots = 12\). The ways to write \(12\) as such a product include \(12\), \(6 \times 2\), \(4 \times 3\), and \(3 \times 2 \times 2\). Each gives a set of exponents to spend on primes.

To keep the number small, give the biggest exponents to the smallest primes. For the split \(3 \times 2 \times 2\), that means \(2^2 \times 3^1 \times 5^1\). Build the number for each split and take the least.
Compare \(2^{11} = 2048\), \(2^5 \times 3 = 96\), \(2^3 \times 3^2 = 72\), and \(2^2 \times 3 \times 5 = 60\). Which is smallest?
blocks.sol: Since the divisor count is \((a+1)(b+1)\cdots\), a number with exactly \(12\) divisors needs exponents whose plus-one values multiply to \(12\). Each way of writing \(12\) as such a product gives a recipe, and to make the number itself small we hand the largest exponents to the smallest primes. $$12 = 12 \ \Rightarrow\ 2^{11} = 2048$$ $$12 = 6 \times 2 \ \Rightarrow\ 2^{5} \times 3 = 96$$ $$12 = 4 \times 3 \ \Rightarrow\ 2^{3} \times 3^{2} = 72$$ $$12 = 3 \times 2 \times 2 \ \Rightarrow\ 2^{2} \times 3 \times 5 = 60$$ The smallest result is \(\boxed{60}\). Quick check, \(60 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 5\) gives \((2+1)(1+1)(1+1) = 3 \times 2 \times 2 = 12\) divisors, and no arrangement of exponents summing to that product lands lower, since spreading the choices across the primes \(2, 3, 5\) is cheaper than stacking them high on \(2\) alone. The general idea is that to hit a target divisor count with the smallest number, you factor the target into exponent-plus-one pieces and load the biggest exponents onto the smallest primes.

blocks.x: \(720=2^4\times3^2\times5\). An even divisor must contain at least one 2. How many even divisors does 720 have?
hints: An even divisor must take at least one \(2\). Normally the \(2\)s offer choices \(0, 1, 2, 3, 4\), but forbidding zero \(2\)s leaves only \(1, 2, 3, 4\). The \(3\)s and the \(5\) keep all their choices.
So the even divisors number \(4 \times (2+1) \times (1+1)\). Alternatively, count all the divisors and subtract the odd ones, which are the divisors of \(3^2 \times 5\).
blocks.sol: An even divisor of \(720 = 2^4 \times 3^2 \times 5\) must carry at least one factor of \(2\). In the counting rule, the \(2\)s normally offer five choices, taking \(0, 1, 2, 3,\) or \(4\) of them, but ruling out the choice of zero leaves only the four choices \(1, 2, 3, 4\). The \(3\)s still offer \(2 + 1 = 3\) choices and the \(5\) still offers \(1 + 1 = 2\), so the even divisors number $$4 \times 3 \times 2 = \boxed{24}.$$ A nice way to confirm it is to count from the other side. All of \(720\) has \((4+1)(2+1)(1+1) = 5 \times 3 \times 2 = 30\) divisors, and the odd ones use no \(2\)s at all, so they are the divisors of \(3^2 \times 5\), numbering \((2+1)(1+1) = 6\). Subtracting, \(30 - 6 = 24\), matching exactly. Quick check, both routes land on \(24\). The general idea is that you can steer the counting rule by restricting one prime's choices, which lets you count special families of divisors like the even ones or the multiples of a given factor.

blocks.x: Highly composite numbers beat every smaller number in divisor count: 1, 2, 4, 6, 12, 24, 36, 60, 120, 360. Civilizations pick these for time and angles because many divisors make sharing easy.

The chapter followed one thread. Look inside a number, find its primes, read the fingerprint, and everything follows, divisibility, perfect powers, GCD, LCM, and the exact divisor count. Chapter 4 turns to fractions.

review.x: A number is given as \(2^4 \times 5^2\). Use the add-one-and-multiply rule to count its positive divisors.
hints: Add one to each exponent and multiply. The \(2\)s give \(4+1\) choices and the \(5\)s give \(2+1\).
review.sol: Each prime offers one more choice than its exponent, so for \(2^4 \times 5^2\) the count is $$(4+1)(2+1) = 5 \times 3 = \boxed{15}.$$ Quick check, the divisors are \(2^i \times 5^j\) with \(i\) from \(0\) to \(4\) and \(j\) from \(0\) to \(2\), which is \(5\) by \(3\) combinations, exactly \(15\). The general idea is that the divisor count is the product of each exponent plus one.

review.x: Factor \(600 = 2^3 \times 3 \times 5^2\) and apply the counting rule. How many positive divisors does 600 have?
hints: With \(600 = 2^3 \times 3^1 \times 5^2\), the choice counts are \(3+1\), \(1+1\), and \(2+1\).
Multiply \(4 \times 2 \times 3\).
review.sol: Reading the fingerprint \(600 = 2^3 \times 3^1 \times 5^2\), the counting rule gives $$(3+1)(1+1)(2+1) = 4 \times 2 \times 3 = \boxed{24}.$$ Quick check, \(600\) shares the count \(24\) with \(360\) from the lesson, since both spread their primes into the same shape of choices. The general idea is that once a number is in fingerprint form, its divisor count is a single multiplication away.

review.x: Which has more divisors, \(64 = 2^6\) or \(90 = 2 \times 3^2 \times 5\)? Count each and enter the number with more.
hints: \(64\) gives \(7\) divisors, while \(90\) gives \(2 \times 3 \times 2 = 12\). Which is larger?
review.sol: A single tall stack counts poorly. For \(64 = 2^6\), the divisor count is \((6+1) = 7\). For \(90 = 2 \times 3^2 \times 5\), it is \((1+1)(2+1)(1+1) = 2 \times 3 \times 2 = 12\). So the smaller-looking \(90\) wins with \(12\) divisors against \(64\)'s \(7\), and the answer is \(\boxed{90}\). Quick check, \(64\) really has only the seven divisors \(1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64\). The general idea is that spreading primes out makes far more divisors than stacking one prime high, so divisor count tracks fingerprint shape, not size.

review.x: How many whole numbers from 1 to 100 have an odd number of divisors?
hints: A number has an odd divisor count exactly when it is a perfect square, since that forces every exponent even. So count the perfect squares from \(1\) to \(100\).
The perfect squares are \(1, 4, 9, \ldots, 100\), the squares of \(1\) through \(10\).
review.sol: A divisor count \((a+1)(b+1)\cdots\) is odd only when every exponent is even, which is the fingerprint of a perfect square. So the numbers with an odd divisor count from \(1\) to \(100\) are exactly the perfect squares there, $$1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100,$$ the squares of \(1\) through \(10\). That is \(\boxed{10}\) numbers. Quick check, \(100 = 2^2 \times 5^2\) has \((2+1)(2+1) = 9\) divisors, odd as expected. The general idea is that odd divisor counts belong to perfect squares and nothing else, so counting them is just counting squares.

review.x: Find the smallest whole number with exactly 8 divisors.
hints: You need \((a+1)(b+1)\cdots = 8\). The ways include \(8\), \(4 \times 2\), and \(2 \times 2 \times 2\), giving exponent sets \(\{7\}\), \(\{3,1\}\), and \(\{1,1,1\}\).
Put the biggest exponents on the smallest primes. Compare \(2^7 = 128\), \(2^3 \times 3 = 24\), and \(2 \times 3 \times 5 = 30\).
review.sol: A number with exactly \(8\) divisors needs exponents whose plus-one values multiply to \(8\). The recipes, each loading the biggest exponents onto the smallest primes, are $$8 = 8 \ \Rightarrow\ 2^7 = 128, \qquad 8 = 4 \times 2 \ \Rightarrow\ 2^3 \times 3 = 24, \qquad 8 = 2 \times 2 \times 2 \ \Rightarrow\ 2 \times 3 \times 5 = 30.$$ The smallest is \(\boxed{24}\). Quick check, \(24 = 2^3 \times 3\) gives \((3+1)(1+1) = 8\) divisors, namely \(1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24\). The general idea is that to reach a target divisor count with the least number, you factor the target and give the largest exponents to the smallest primes.

review.x: How many positive divisors does \(2^{10}\) have?
hints: A power of a single prime, \(p^a\), has its divisors \(p^0, p^1, \ldots, p^a\). How many is that?
The exponent runs from \(0\) up to \(10\), so add one to the exponent.
review.sol: A power of a single prime \(2^{10}\) has divisors \(2^0, 2^1, \ldots, 2^{10}\), one for each exponent from \(0\) to \(10\). The counting rule says the same, \((10+1) = \boxed{11}\). Quick check, the list \(1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024\) holds exactly eleven numbers. The general idea is that a prime raised to the power \(a\) has \(a+1\) divisors, the simplest case of the rule.

review.x: A number has exactly three divisors only when it is the square of a prime. How many numbers from 1 to 50 have exactly three divisors?
hints: For the divisor count \((a+1)(b+1)\cdots\) to equal \(3\), and since \(3\) is prime, the product must be a single factor \(3\). That means one prime with exponent \(2\) and no others.
So the number must be the square of a prime, \(p^2\). List the prime squares up to \(50\).
review.sol: The divisor count \((a+1)(b+1)\cdots\) equals \(3\) only when the product is the single factor \(3\), since \(3\) is prime and cannot be split into smaller whole-number factors above \(1\). That forces exactly one prime with exponent \(2\) and nothing else, so the number must be the square of a prime, \(p^2\), whose divisors are just \(1\), \(p\), and \(p^2\). The prime squares up to \(50\) are $$2^2 = 4,\quad 3^2 = 9,\quad 5^2 = 25,\quad 7^2 = 49,$$ since \(11^2 = 121\) overshoots. That is \(\boxed{4}\) numbers. Quick check, \(25\) has exactly the three divisors \(1, 5, 25\). The general idea is that a number has exactly three divisors precisely when it is the square of a prime, a clean fingerprint forced by the counting rule.

review.x: How many of 360's divisors are even, given \(360 = 2^3 \times 3^2 \times 5\)?
hints: An even divisor must take at least one \(2\), so the \(2\)s offer choices \(1, 2, 3\) instead of \(0, 1, 2, 3\). That is \(3\) choices. The \(3\)s and the \(5\) keep their full counts.
Even divisors number \(3 \times (2+1) \times (1+1)\). Or count all \(24\) divisors and subtract the odd ones, the divisors of \(3^2 \times 5\).
review.sol: An even divisor of \(360 = 2^3 \times 3^2 \times 5\) must include at least one factor of \(2\). The \(2\)s normally give four choices, \(0, 1, 2, 3\), but ruling out zero leaves three, \(1, 2, 3\). The \(3\)s give \(2 + 1 = 3\) choices and the \(5\) gives \(1 + 1 = 2\), so the even divisors number $$3 \times 3 \times 2 = \boxed{18}.$$ Checking from the other side, all of \(360\) has \(4 \times 3 \times 2 = 24\) divisors, and the odd ones are the divisors of \(3^2 \times 5\), numbering \(3 \times 2 = 6\), so even ones are \(24 - 6 = 18\), a match. Quick check, both routes give \(18\). The general idea is that requiring a prime to appear shrinks that prime's choice count by one, which counts a chosen family of divisors directly.

review.x: Divisor counting rule. For \(n=p^a q^b\cdots\), count is \((a+1)(b+1)\cdots\). Depends on exponent shape, not size. Odd count ↔ perfect square. Trim one prime's choices for a sub-family. Run backward to design a number with a target count.
Locker \(n\) is flipped once per divisor, so it ends open only when it has an odd divisor count, exactly the perfect squares. Open lockers: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100.

blocks.x: Chapter 3 looked inside numbers for factors and ended with a master key, the prime fingerprint. Factors set the stage, divisibility tests gave quick eyes, primes were atoms, and GCD, LCM, and divisor counts all followed.

Factors and multiples are one relationship read from two ends. A factor of \(n\) is a side of a rectangle of area \(n\), so factors arrive in pairs that multiply to \(n\), \(d\) with its partner \(n \div d\). Turn the arrow around and the small number is a factor while the large one is a multiple. From this came several facts. A number has finitely many factors but infinitely many multiples, \(1\) divides everything, every number is both a factor and a multiple of itself, and \(0\) is a multiple of every number. And a number has an odd factor count exactly when it is a perfect square, the lone case where a factor pairs with itself.

Closure and chaining. Add or subtract two multiples of \(n\) and you land on a multiple of \(n\) again, since the shared \(n\) factors out, so a stray non-multiple can never be added onto a multiple to reach one. That is closure. And if \(a\) is a factor of \(b\), then every multiple of \(b\) is automatically a multiple of \(a\), since the multiple carries \(b\) inside it and \(b\) carries \(a\). That is chaining, and it runs only downward toward the smaller factors. These two ideas powered every divisibility test that followed.

With that structure in hand, the next question was practical. Must you divide a big number out the long way to know whether some small number goes into it, or can you glance at the digits and simply know? Place value gave the answer.

Divisibility tests all spring from place value. Cut a number where one chunk becomes a multiple of the divisor, let closure throw that chunk away, and judge the small piece left behind. The last digit settles \(2\), \(5\), \(10\), since every higher place is a multiple of ten. The last two digits settle \(4\) and the last three settle \(8\), since \(100 = 4 \times 25\) and \(1000 = 8 \times 125\). The digit sum settles \(9\) and \(3\), since every power of ten is one above a run of nines. The alternating digit sum settles \(11\), since the powers of ten bounce just below then above its multiples. Run any test backward and the same cut recovers a hidden digit.

Combine tests only along coprime factors. Split a composite divisor into coprime parts and test each. A split like \(12 = 2 \times 6\) fails because 2 and 6 share a factor, so passing both tests would count that shared factor twice while 12 demands it twice.
Testing for small factors led naturally to the smallest building blocks of all, the numbers that have no factors to find.

Primes are the atoms. A prime has exactly two divisors, a composite has more, 1 is neither, and 2 is the only even prime. To test primality, divide by primes up to \(\sqrt{n}\) and stop, any factor above \(\sqrt{n}\) has a smaller partner already checked.

Every number has one unique prime fingerprint (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). Written with exponents like \(360 = 2^3 \times 3^2 \times 5\), it reveals: perfect square ↔ all exponents even, perfect cube ↔ all multiples of 3, trailing zeros = count of 2-and-5 pairs. Primes never run out.
Once a single number could be broken into its atoms, two numbers could be compared atom by atom, which opened the last ideas of the chapter.

GCD and LCM are two readings of two fingerprints. GCD takes each shared prime to its lower power. LCM takes every prime to its higher power. They satisfy \(\gcd\times\operatorname{lcm}=a\times b\). Numbers sharing no prime are coprime, and common multiples of a pair are multiples of their lcm.

The fingerprint counts divisors too. For \(n = p^a q^b \cdots\), the divisor count is \((a+1)(b+1)\cdots\). It depends on exponent shape, not size, so a smaller number can out-divide a larger one. The count is odd exactly for perfect squares.

The prime fingerprint settled divisibility, perfect powers, GCD, LCM, and divisor counts throughout the chapter. Chapter 4 turns to fractions, and these tools come straight along: GCD reduces fractions, LCM gives common denominators.
